perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Receiving Party may publicly disclose (i) Confidential Information of the type set forth in clause (iv) of Section 1 (a) above and (ii) the fact that the Parties are or have been discussing a proposed Transaction and/or the terms of such proposed Transaction, in each case, only if and to the extent that the Receiving Party determines in good faith based on an opinion of legal counsel that it is required to do so under applicable law or subpoena or other legal process in connection with a Transaction, provided that the Receiving Party furnishes a copy of the proposed disclosure ORCC not less than five business days prior to such disclosure (unless the disclosing Party determines in good faith that it is not reasonably practicable to give such advance notice) and considers in good faith ORCC’s comments on such proposed disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Receiving Party may publicly disclose (i) Confidential Information of the type set forth in clause (iv) of Section 1 (a) above and (ii) the fact that the Parties are or have been discussing a proposed Transaction and/or the terms of such proposed Transaction, in each case, only if and to the extent that the Receiving Party determines in good faith based on an opinion of legal counsel that it is required to do so under applicable law or subpoena or other legal process in connection with a Transaction, provided that the Receiving Party furnishes a copy of the proposed disclosure ORCC not less than one business day prior to such disclosure (unless the disclosing Party determines in good faith that it is not reasonably practicable to give such advance notice) and considers in good faith ORCC’s comments on such proposed disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for public disclosure of confidential information to one business day may be insufficient to allow ORCC to seek a protective order, potentially violating securities regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits making materially false or misleading statements or omissions that could influence investors. Reducing the notice period increases the risk of premature or inaccurate disclosure of transaction information, which could violate this rule.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notice period for disclosing confidential information from five business days to one. The Perturbation Explanation states this may not allow sufficient time to seek a protective order, potentially violating securities regulations. The Law Explanation states that Rule 10b-5 prohibits misleading statements that could influence investors, and the reduced notice period increases the risk of premature disclosure. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, explicitly prohibits making untrue statements of material fact or omitting material facts necessary to prevent statements from being misleading in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. Reducing the time for review and potential intervention directly increases the risk of such misleading disclosures, creating a contradiction. Therefore, this falls under Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction, where the time-sensitive obligation violates federal law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall control and govern all Confidential Information disclosed by ORCC to Receiving Party from and after the Effective Date (as defined above) for a period of one (1) year (the “Term”) unless earlier terminated by the Parties in a separate agreement or instrument executed by both Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall control and govern all Confidential Information disclosed by ORCC to Receiving Party from and after the Effective Date (as defined above) for a period of ninety (90) days (the “Term”) unless earlier terminated by the Parties in a separate agreement or instrument executed by both Parties.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality term to 90 days may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, contradicting state laws providing longer protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: DUTSA protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret. A 90-day term may be unreasonably short and fail to provide adequate protection, especially considering the potential lifespan of some trade secrets.

Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not b
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality term from one year to 90 days. The Law Explanation states that 'DUTSA protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret. A 90-day term may be unreasonably short and fail to provide adequate protection.' Scraped Law Snippet 1, defining 'trade secret,' indicates that it 'derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known.' If a trade secret maintains its value beyond 90 days, the reduced term contradicts the intention to protect it, as the 90-day limit may be insufficient, leading to potential misappropriation after that period.

perturbation.original_text: Promptly upon the expiration or termination of this Agreement, or with respect to Technical Information only, upon. ORCC’s reasonable written request, Receiving Party shall return or destroy all written material (if any) containing or reflecting any Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the expiration or termination of this Agreement, Receiving Party may retain all written material (if any) containing or reflecting any Confidential Information, without any obligation to return or destroy such materials, unless otherwise agreed to in writing.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing indefinite retention of confidential information after the agreement's termination contradicts data protection laws and principles of fair competition.
perturbation.law_explanation: GDPR Article 5(1)(e) requires that personal data be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed. Indefinite retention violates this principle.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 5 GDPR – Principles relating to processing of personal data - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Personal data shall be: processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner in relation to the data subject (‘lawfulness, fairness and transparency’); collected for specified, explicit and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner that is incompatible with those purposes; further processing for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes shall, in accordance with Article 89 (1), not be considered to be incompatible with the initial purposes (‘purpose limitation’); adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which they are processed (‘data minimisation’); accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date; every reasonable step must be taken to ensure that personal data that are inaccurate, having regard to the purposes for which they are processed, are erased or rectified without delay (‘accuracy’); kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed; personal data may be stored for longer periods insofar as the personal data will be processed solely for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes in accordance with Article 89( 1) subject to implementation of the appropriate technical and organisational measures required by this Regulation in order to safeguard the rights and freedoms of the data subject (‘storage limitation’); processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage, using appropriate technical or organisational measures (‘integrity and confidentiality’). The controller shall be responsible for, and be able to demonstrate compliance with, paragraph 1 (‘accountability’). Suitable Recitals ( 39 ) Principles of Data Processing ( 74 ) Responsibility and Liability of the Controller ← Art. 4 GDPR Art. 6 GDPR → GDPR Table of contents Report error
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the principles of data protection outlined in GDPR Article 5(1)(e), specifically regarding storage limitation. The original text mandates the return or destruction of confidential information upon termination of the agreement. However, the changed text permits the Receiving Party to retain such information indefinitely, unless otherwise agreed in writing. This directly violates the GDPR's requirement to keep personal data only as long as necessary for its intended purpose, as highlighted in the provided snippet: 'kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed'. The changed text removes this obligation, creating a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: For one (1) year from the Effective Date, Receiving Party (including its subsidiaries and affiliates) shall not directly or indirectly solicit, hire or engage ORCC’s employees with whom Receiving Party comes into contact, or of whom it becomes aware, in connection with this Agreement or a Transaction and whose annual base salary from ORCC exceeds $150,000, except with ORCC’s prior written consent; provided, however, that the foregoing restriction shall not apply to the hiring of ORCC’s employees who may respond to internet or other advertisements of general circulation and not specifically targeted at such employees or any action in which no Representative of Receiving Party is involved or aware of the existence of this Agreement or discussions of a possible Transaction.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party (including its subsidiaries and affiliates) shall not directly or indirectly solicit, hire or engage ORCC’s employees with whom Receiving Party comes into contact, or of whom it becomes aware, in connection with this Agreement or a Transaction, regardless of their annual base salary from ORCC, and this restriction shall remain in effect perpetually, except with ORCC’s prior written consent; provided, however, that the foregoing restriction shall not apply to the hiring of ORCC’s employees who may respond to internet or other advertisements of general circulation and not specifically targeted at such employees or any action in which no Representative of Receiving Party is involved or aware of the existence of this Agreement or discussions of a possible Transaction.
perturbation.explanation: Imposing a perpetual non-solicitation clause, regardless of salary, is likely an unreasonable restraint on trade and violates employee rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 16600 invalidates contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. A perpetual non-solicitation agreement with no regard to salary is an unreasonable restraint and likely unenforceable in California, especially since ORCC is incorporated in Delaware.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a perpetual non-solicitation agreement without salary considerations, contradicting California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. Snippet 1 states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." The modified contract restrains ORCC's employees, regardless of salary, from being solicited or hired indefinitely, acting as an unreasonable restraint on trade. This modification undermines employee rights and imposes an unenforceable restriction under California law, which deems contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession void, thus creating a contradiction.
